coredata.dc:title: Glucose-sensitive colorimetric sensor based on peroxidase mimics activity of carbon dots-functionalized Fe3O4 nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Jinjin
Zhao, Binyu
Luo, Xiaogang
Wu, Fengshou
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the carbon dots-functionalized Fe3O4 nanocomposites (CDs@Fe3O4) were successfully prepared by a facile and cost-effective method. The as-prepared nanocomposites were characterized using several analytical techniques including Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Compared with that of bare Fe3O4 nanoparticles, CDs@Fe3O4 exhibited the enhanced affinity toward hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and higher peroxidase-like activity, which could efficiently catalyze the oxidation of 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2. The mechanism for the high peroxidase mimics activity of CDs@Fe3O4 was investigated with isopropanol, P-benzoquinone and D-histidine as reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavengers. The results suggested that the CDs@Fe3O4 could effectively catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals (·OH), singlet oxygen (1O2) and superoxide radicals (O2 −). Based on these phenomena, we developed a facile colorimetric method for the detection of H2O2 and glucose, and their limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.53 μM and 2.2 μM respectively. Finally, the colorimetric method based on CDs@Fe3O4 was successfully applied to blood glucose detection, demonstrating the potential application in the biological area. Thus, this work provided a new strategy to construct artificial nanozyme for colorimetric sensing of H2O2 and related molecules in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3O4
Peroxidase mimics
Colorimetric
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CDs@Fe3O4
Steady-state dynamics analysis of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CDs@Fe3O4
Measurement of human blood glucose by CDs@Fe3O4
Repeatability of peroxidase activity of CDs@Fe3O4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles (a) and CDs@Fe3O4 (b); (B) IR spectra of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (a) and CDs@Fe3O4 (b); (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (a), Fe3O4 nanoparticles (b) and CDs@Fe3O4 (c).

(A) Absorbance changes of TMB at 652 nm in different reaction systems: (a) Fe3O4 + TMB, (b) CDs@Fe3O4 + TMB, (c) Fe3O4 + TMB + H2O2, (d) CD@Fe3O4 + TMB+ H2O2 incubated at 40 °C in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 3.8); (B) the visible color change of the different systems after incubation for 15 min.
The peroxidase-like activity of the CDs@Fe3O4 at different pH values, temperature, incubation time, and H2O2 concentration (a-d).
The peroxidase-like activity of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles at different pH values, temperature, incubation time, and H2O2 concentration (a-d).

Steady-state kinetic assay and catalytic mechanism of CDs@Fe3O4. Data was shown as mean ± SEM (n = 3 replicates). Statistics were performed by a one-way ANOVA (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001).

Steady-state kinetic assay and catalytic mechanism of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Data was shown as mean ± SEM (n = 3 replicates). Statistics were performed by a one-way ANOVA (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001).

The absorbance change of the CDs@Fe3O4 + TMB at 652 nm with the concentration of H2O2. Inset: photographs of the colored products at different concentrations of H2O2. Data was shown as mean ± SEM (n = 3 replicates). Statistics were performed by a one-way ANOVA (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001).

(a) Determination of reactive oxygen species in CDs with D-histidine, isopropyl alcohol and p-benzoquinone as ROS scavengers; (b) Reactive oxygen species produced by Fe3O4 nanoparticles with TA as ROS sensor; (c) Reactive oxygen species generation of CDs@Fe3O4 with TA as ROS sensor; (d) Determination of reactive oxygen species in CDs@Fe3O4 with D-histidine, isopropyl alcohol and p-benzoquinone as ROS scavengers.

(a) The linear calibration plots for glucose detection; (b) Selectivity of the CDs@Fe3O4 detecting colorimetric platform for glucose detection; (C) UV–Vis spectra of buffer solution containing (a) TMB + CDs@Fe3O4 + diluted human serum-GOx system and (b) TMB + CDs@Fe3O4 system; (d) The catalytic activity of CDs@Fe3O4 during the five consecutive cycles. Inset: the color change for the different samples. Data was shown as mean ± SEM (n = 3 replicates). Statistics were performed by a one-way ANOVA (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001).
Mechanism test of CDs@Fe3O4 catalyzing H2O2 to produce ROS.
Comparison of the apparent Michaelis-Mentenconstant (Km) and maximum reaction rate (Vm) of different enzyme mimics.
Comparison with other state-of-the-art catalysts for glucose detection from the linear range and detection limit.

head.ce:title: Glucose-sensitive colorimetric sensor based on peroxidase mimics activity of carbon dots-functionalized Fe3O4 nanocomposites

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the carbon dots-functionalized Fe3O4 nanocomposites (CDs@Fe3O4) were successfully prepared by a facile and cost-effective method. The as-prepared nanocomposites were characterized using several analytical techniques including Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). Compared with that of bare Fe3O4 nanoparticles, CDs@Fe3O4 exhibited the enhanced affinity toward hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and higher peroxidase-like activity, which could efficiently catalyze the oxidation of 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into blue ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2. The mechanism for the high peroxidase mimics activity of CDs@Fe3O4 was investigated with isopropanol, P-benzoquinone and D-histidine as reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavengers. The results suggested that the CDs@Fe3O4 could effectively catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals (·OH), singlet oxygen (1O2) and superoxide radicals (O2 −). Based on these phenomena, we developed a facile colorimetric method for the detection of H2O2 and glucose, and their limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.53 μM and 2.2 μM respectively. Finally, the colorimetric method based on CDs@Fe3O4 was successfully applied to blood glucose detection, demonstrating the potential application in the biological area. Thus, this work provided a new strategy to construct artificial nanozyme for colorimetric sensing of H2O2 and related molecules in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural enzymes have attracted wide attention in the fields of environmental protection, and disease diagnosis and treatment because of their highly catalytic activity in the mild condition [1,2]. However, the lack of stability and inactivity in harsh environments significantly limited their practical application [3]. Therefore, it was of great significance to construct enzyme mimics with high efficiency and stability. Among these materials, Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) are a kind of excellent mimic enzymes [4], which possessed the advantages of small particle size [5], high sensitivity [6], low toxicity [7], strong magnetic response and easy access to raw materials [8], therefore showing the potential applications in industry [9], biomedicine and other fields [10]. In recent years, several studies demonstrated that the Fe3O4-based hybrid composites with clear structures exhibited the inherent peroxide-like activity of Fe3O4 and unique properties of other components [11,12]. For example, the combination of Fe3O4 with enzymes or metal catalysts showed enhanced catalytic and separating capacity activity [13]. However, Fe3O4 MNPs have the shortcomings of poor dispersion stability and biocompatibility, which greatly reduced their magnetism and catalytic activity [14,15]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop the nanozymes with high catalytic activity, superior stability, and ease of separation.

In addition to metal oxides, a variety of carbon-based or organic nanomaterials with low cost and high stability were developed as artificial peroxidase mimics [16,17], such as metal organic framework (MOF) [18,19], covalent organic framework [20,21] and carbon dots (CDs) [22,23]. Among of them, CDs had attracted wide attention due to its particle size <10 nm, high water solubility, good biocompatibility, and excellent optical properties [24,25]. Therefore, the functionalization of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with CDs might improve the dispersion stability and biocompatibility of Fe3O4 nanoparticles.

Herein, we reported a facile one-step preparation of the CDs-functionalized Fe3O4 nanocomposites (CDs@Fe3O4). Compared with bare Fe3O4 nanoparticles, CDs@Fe3O4 showed the inherent peroxide-like activity, higher catalytic activity, and better dispersity. Furthermore, CDs@Fe3O4 can efficiently catalyze the oxidation of substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramylbenzidine (TMB) into ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2, resulting in a visible color change in a few minutes. The catalytic mechanism study suggested that the peroxidase-mimicking activity of CDs@Fe3O4 was probably ascribed to the catalytic transformation of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) into the hydroxyl radicals (·OH), singlet oxygen (1O2) and superoxide radicals (O2 •-). Finally, the CDs@Fe3O4 was applied for the quantitative detection of glucose in the serum samples.

The synthetic procedures of CDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles were shown in the supporting information. The procedure for the preparation of CDs@Fe3O4 was shown in Scheme 1 . Briefly, CDs (20 mg) and Fe3O4 nanoparticles (0.02 g) were dispersed in DI water (10 mL). After refluxing for 12 h, the nanocomposites were collected using a bar magnet. The obtained solid was washed thoroughly with deionized water and dried in oven for 48 h.

The peroxidase-like activity of CDs@Fe3O4 colorimetric assay was evaluated with TMB and H2O2 as substrates. The chromogenic substance TMB could be oxidized by H2O2 to produce a product with blue color (ox-TMB), along with the appearance of absorption at 652 nm [26]. In a typical reaction, 100 μL of 1 mg/mL CDs@Fe3O4, 100 μL of H2O2 (10 mM), and 100 μL of TMB (5 mM) were added to the NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 3.8) with a total reaction volume of 3 mL. The kinetic parameters (Vm and Km) were calculated according to Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot.
(1)
1 / V =
K m
/
V m
1 /
S
+ 1 /
K m
where V refers to the initial velocity, Vm refers to the maximum reaction velocity, [S] is the concentration of the substrate, and Km is the Michaelis constant [27].

The Glucose detection was carried out according to the following procedure. Firstly, 0.1 mL glucose oxidase (1 mg/mL) and 0.5 mL d-Glucose (0.01 mM–0.5 mM) were incubated in PBS buffer solution at 37 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, 0.1 mL TMB (5 mM), 0.1 mL CDs@Fe3O4 and 2.2 mL NaAc-HAc buffer solution (pH = 3.8) were added to the above solution. After incubation at 40 °C, the standard curve was measured.

To verify the detailed catalytic mechanism of CDs@Fe3O4, the isopropanol, D-histidine and P-benzoquinone were used as ROS scavengers to track the 1O2, ·OH, and O2 •-, respectively [28]. Briefly, 100 μL scavenger was added into the reaction system, which contained 0.1 mL of TMB (5 mM), 0.1 mL of CDs@Fe3O4 (1 mg/mL), 0.1 mL of H2O2 (10 mM) and 2.7 mL NaAc-HAc buffer solution (pH = 3.8). After incubation for 10 min, the absorbance at 652 nm was recorded. Meanwhile, the ·OH production was verified using terephthalic acid (TA) as sensor [29]. Specifically, different concentrations of CDs@Fe3O4, 10 mM H2O2, and 0.5 mM TA were cultured in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 3.8) at 40 °C for 10 min [30]. The fluorescence intensity of the system was then observed with the concentration of CDs@Fe3O4.

CDs@Fe3O4 was prepared by a simple acid-base condensation reaction, in which CDs provided carboxyl group while Fe3O4 nanoparticles provided amino group. The ratio of the two reactants was adjusted to obtain the CDs@Fe3O4 with the highest yield (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CDs@Fe3O4
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structure of Fe3O4 and CDs@Fe3O4 was evaluated through XRD spectrum. As shown in Fig. 1A, the XRD pattern of Fe3O4 nanoparticles exhibited the diffraction peaks at 30.1°, 35.3°, 43.2°, 53.5°, 56.8° and 62.7°, corresponding to the [220], [311], [400], [422], [511] and [440] crystallographic planes, respectively [31]. The XRD pattern of CDs@Fe3O4 showed that the crystalline cubic spinel structure of Fe3O4 nanoparticles did not display any significant change after modification with CDs. Comparing with that of bare Fe3O4, a new peak at 20.0 was observed in CDs@Fe3O4, indicating the success of formation between CDs and Fe3O4.

The functional groups of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CDs@Fe3O4 were verified through Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1B). Compared with that of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the peak at 1680 cm−1 was observed in CDs@Fe3O4, which could be assigned to the stretching vibration of amide bond [32]. The peak at 3500 cm−1 can be ascribed to the vibration peak of -NH2, whereas the peak at 590 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of FeO bond [33]. These observations indicated the successful coordination between Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, Fe3O4 nanoparticles, and CDs@Fe3O4 were recorded in DI water. As shown in Fig. 1C, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles exhibited no visible absorbance between 300 and 800 nm, while CDs@Fe3O4 exhibited the obvious absorption at 420 nm, indicating the success of interaction between CDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The dispersion stability of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CDs@Fe3O4 was verified by comparing the dispersion of two materials in DI water every 10 min. As shown in Fig. S1, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles showed the obvious stratification after storage for half an hour, while CDs@Fe3O4 remained suspended, suggesting the improvement of dispersion stability after functionalization.

The microstructure of the as-prepared CDs@Fe3O4 was characterized using TEM and SEM. As shown in Fig. S2a, the SEM images of CDs@Fe3O4 were similar to that of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, with the particle size about 60 nm. Besides, the surface of CDs@Fe3O4 was rougher than that of bare Fe3O4, probably due to the surface adsorption of CDs (Fig. S2b). The TEM images (Fig. S2c and S2d) suggested that the particle size of CDs@Fe3O4 was significantly larger than that of pure Fe3O4 nanoparticles, further confirming the functionalization of CDs on the surface of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The presence of O and Fe elements in CDs@Fe3O4 was verified by the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). (As shown in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4).

Both CDs@Fe3O4 and Fe3O4 nanoparticles could catalyze the oxidization of TMB to ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2 with the appearance of a maximum absorbance at 652 nm, which was similar with the peroxidase activity of enzymes such as the common HRP enzyme [34]. To investigate the role of CDs in the peroxidase activity of CDs@Fe3O4, we compared the catalytic activity of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CDs@Fe3O4 under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 2A, the absorbance of the CDs@Fe3O4 + TMB + H2O2 system at 652 nm was higher than that of other systems, demonstrating the necessity of both CDs@Fe3O4 and H2O2 for its peroxidase-like activity. Moreover, the visible color change of the CD@Fe3O4 + TMB + H2O2 system was more obvious than that of the Fe3O4 + TMB + H2O2 system under same condition (Fig. 2B), indicating the higher catalytic activity of CDs@Fe3O4. Thus, the surface modification of Fe3O4 nanoparticles by CDs could enhance its peroxidase activity, probably through a synergistic mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state dynamics analysis of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and CDs@Fe3O4
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetic mechanism of CDs@Fe3O4 was further investigated by changing the concentrations of H2O2 and TMB. The kinetics experiments were performed in a total reaction volume of 3.0 mL NaAc-HAc buffer solution (pH 3.8), which contained 1 mg/mL CDs@Fe3O4, 5 mM TMB and 33.5 mM H2O2 (unless otherwise specified). By monitoring the absorbance changes of the system at 652 nm, the typical Michaelis Menten curve was obtained (Fig. 5 ).

Besides, we studied the kinetic mechanism of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 6 ). The parameters of Km and Vm were obtained through the Michaelis-Menten model to evaluate the substrate-enzyme binding affinity. As shown in Table 1 , the Km value of CDs@Fe3O4 was measured to be 3.16 mM with H2O2 as substrate from Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot, which was significantly lower than that of HRP (3.70 mM). In addition, with TMB as substrate, the Km value was calculated to be 0.17 mM. As indicated in Table 1, the lowest K m value of CDs@Fe3O4 indicated its highest substrate affinity compared to other catalyst, revealing that CDs@Fe3O4 possessed high affinity toward H2O2 and TMB.

From the above optimization experiment, we confirmed that the catalytic activity of CDs@Fe3O4 was related to the H2O2 concentration. As shown in Fig. 7 , the absorbance of the TMB + H2O2 + CDs@Fe3O4 system at 652 nm was proportional to the concentration of H2O2 ranging from 2.5 to 33.5 μM. Based on this calibration curve, the LOD was calculated to be 1.53 × 10−6 M, lower than most of the reported materials [37]. Meanwhile, the corresponding color of solution was turning even more green, which could be obviously observed with the naked eye.

The peroxidase-mimicking activity of CDs@Fe3O4 was probably ascribed to its catalytic performance, which could transform H2O2 to reactive oxygen species (ROS), including hydroxyl radicals (·OH), singlet oxygen (1O2), and superoxide anions (O2 •-). To verify the detailed mechanism, the terephthalic acid (TA) was added as a fluorescent probe into the H2O2 + CDs@Fe3O4 system, which could react with •OH to form highly fluorescent 2-hydroxyl terephthalic acid under acidic conditions. Meanwhile, the isopropanol, P-benzoquinone and D-histidine were used as ROS scavengers to track ·OH, O2 •-, and 1O2 respectively (Scheme 2 ).

As shown in Fig. 8a, the absorbance of the CDs + TMB + H2O2 system at 652 nm obviously decreased when the ROS scavengers were added, indicating not only •OH was produced, but also O2 − and 1O2. Besides, when the TA was added to the H2O2 + Fe3O4 system, the fluorescence was obviously enhanced, indicating the generation of •OH (Fig. 8b). Similar with that of bare Fe3O4 nanoparticles, the fluorescence intensity of the H2O2 + CDs@Fe3O4 + TA system increased gradually with the concentration of catalyst (Fig. 8c), indicating the generation of •OH induced by CDs@Fe3O4. Meanwhile, the absorbance of the CDs@Fe3O4 + TMB + H2O2 system at 652 nm was recorded with the addition of three ROS scavengers. As shown in Fig. 8d, the absorbance of the system at 652 nm decreased significantly upon the addition of the three ROS scavengers, indicating the generation of O2 −, 1O2 and •OH, which was similar with that of CDs. Therefore, only •OH was generated in the unmodified Fe3O4. However, the CDs@Fe3O4 could catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to produce O2 −, 1O2 and •OH, which accelerated the oxidation capacity of TMB.

Glucose could be oxidized to gluconic acid, O2 and H2O2 in the presence of glucose oxidase. According to this reaction, the detection method for H2O2 can be established to indirectly detect the content of glucose. As shown in Fig. 9a, a good linear relationship between glucose concentration (0–0.5 mM) and the absorbance of the CDs@Fe3O4 + Glucose + TMB system at 652 nm was obtained. Based on this linear relationship, the LOD of glucose was calculated to be 2.2 × 10−6 M, which was comparable to that of report catalysts (Table 2 ).

The selectivity of CDs@Fe3O4 for glucose detection was studied with buffer, sucrose, maltose, fructose and lactose as interfering substances. As indicated in Fig. 9b, the absorbance of the CDs@Fe3O4 + TMB + various sugars system at 652 nm did not show any significant changes even if their concentration was elevated to 10 times higher than that of glucose. Furthermore, we also investigated the selectivity of CDs@Fe3O4 toward metal cations. As shown in Fig. S5, the absorbance of the CDs@Fe3O4 + TMB system at 652 nm exhibited no significant variation after adding metal cations. Therefore, the established colorimetric method possessed the high selectivity for the determination of glucose. This high selectivity of CDs@Fe3O4 enables it to be used to detect the glucose content in human blood.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of human blood glucose by CDs@Fe3O4
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of glucose in real blood serum is important for diabetes diagnosis. Thus, the feasibility of the colorimetric method based on CDs@Fe3O4 was investigated for the determination of blood glucose concentration in human serum. As indicated in Fig. 9c, the absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm was significantly improved after the addition of diluted human serum. According to the calibration curve, the glucose concentration in the normal serum sample was valued as 4.51 mM, which was almost consistent with the glucose concentration measured by the pathological laboratory standard method (5.14 mM). In general, the range of blood glucose concentrations for healthy people and diabetics are 3–8 mM and 9–40 mM, respectively. Thus, we can use this simple colorimetric method to monitor blood sugar levels in diabetics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability of peroxidase activity of CDs@Fe3O4
ce:section.ce:para: The repeatability of CDs@Fe3O4 was evaluated during five cycles of usage. The cyclic catalysis experiments were carried out and their catalytic activity was investigated in each cycle. In this system, 0.1 mL of CDs@Fe3O4 (1 mg/mL), 0.1 mL of H2O2 (10 mM), and 0.1 mL of TMB (5 mM) were added to the buffer solution (3 mL, pH = 3.8). The absorbance of the above solution at 652 nm was observed after incubation for 10 min. After removing the supernatant, the same amount of H2O2 and TMB were added and the absorbance was recorded. As shown in Fig. 9d, after 5 consecutive cycles, the absorbance of the CDs@Fe3O4 + TMB + H2O2 system did not exhibit any significant decrease, indicating the high stability and recyclability of CDs@Fe3O4.

In summary, CDs@Fe3O4 was fabricated for sensitive and selective detection of glucose based on its highly intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. These nanocomposites exhibited high peroxidase-like activity compared with that of bare Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The catalytic activity of CDs@Fe3O4 is affected by pH value, temperature, catalyst dosage and H2O2 concentration. The fluorescence probe and ROS scavengers including isopropanol, P-benzoquinone, and D-histidine were used to explore the catalytic mechanism of CDs@Fe3O4. The result suggested the CDs@Fe3O4 can effectively catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into •OH, O2 − and 1O2. As a new type of peroxidase mimics, CDs@Fe3O4 have many advantages over natural enzymes and other alternatives, such as simple preparation, low cost, good stability, high selectivity, high sensitivity, and easy separation. Therefore, these advantages may facilitate the application of CDs@Fe3O4 in medical diagnostics, biochemistry, and environmental chemistry.

Jinjin Zeng: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Binyu Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Xiaogang Luo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Fengshou Wu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful for the support from Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (grant no. 2022CFC034), Outstanding Young and Middle-aged Scientific Innovation Team of Colleges and Universities of Hubei Province: “Biomass chemical technologies and materials” (grant no. T201908), and the Innovation Project of Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry (grant no. 2023BEEA06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.109914.
